title: Collaborative Git & GitHub
content:
# 🤝 Collaborative Git: GitHub, Remotes, and Team Workflows Git alone is just a local database. The true power of Git emerges when teams connect their local repositories through **Remotes**. A Remote is simply a version of your project hosted somewhere else, like GitHub, GitLab, or an internal corporate server. In this lesson, we master the professional workflows that allow hundreds of engineers to work on the exact same codebase simultaneously without destroying it. --- ## 1️⃣ Cloning vs. Remote Add There are two primary ways to connect your local machine to GitHub. ### Scenario A: The Project Already Exists on GitHub If you join a new company, the code is already on GitHub. You need to download it and automatically set up the connection. ```bash git clone https://github.com/company/project.git cd project ``` This single command downloads the files, initializes the `.git` folder, and automatically sets up a remote called `origin` pointing back to GitHub. ### Scenario B: You Built It Locally First You started a project on your laptop, and now you want to back it up to a shiny new, empty GitHub repository. ```bash git init git add . git commit -m "initial commit" # Link the local database to the cloud URL and name it "origin" git remote add origin https://github.com/yourname/new-project.git # Push the "main" branch to "origin", and set it as the default tracking branch git push -u origin main ``` --- ## 2️⃣ Fetch, Pull, and Push When collaborating, you are constantly syncing your local database with GitHub's database. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Local participant GitHub participant CoWorker CoWorker->>GitHub: git push (adds new feature) Local->>GitHub: git fetch (downloads knowledge of the feature) Note over Local: The knowledge is stored locally,
but your files remain unchanged. Local->>Local: git merge origin/main (updates your files) Note over Local: "git pull" does both fetch and merge
in one command. Local->>GitHub: git push (uploads your changes) ``` 1. **`git push`**: Takes all new commits on your local branch and blasts them up to the corresponding branch on GitHub. 2. **`git fetch`**: Only downloads the *metadata* from GitHub. If a coworker pushed 10 commits, `fetch` downloads them into your `.git` database, but it does **not** change the files in your Working Directory. You can inspect their code before deciding to merge it. 3. **`git pull`**: The dangerous one. It is literally `git fetch` followed immediately by `git merge`. It downloads the new commits and instantly modifies your Working Directory files to include them. --- ## 3️⃣ The Feature Branch Workflow In a professional setting, **you never, ever commit directly to the `main` branch.** The `main` branch represents code currently running in production for live users. You must protect it at all costs. **The Workflow:** 1. A new ticket comes in: "Add stripe payment button." 2. You create an isolated branch: `git checkout -b feature/stripe-payment`. 3. You write the code, make 5 commits locally. 4. You push your branch to GitHub: `git push origin feature/stripe-payment`. 5. On GitHub, you open a **Pull Request (PR)**. ### What is a Pull Request? A Pull Request (or Merge Request in GitLab) is a formal request asking the Lead Developer: "Please pull my `feature/stripe` code and merge it into `main`." A PR creates a discussion thread. Teammates can review the exact lines of code changed, comment on them, demand changes, or click **Approve**. Once approved, it is mathematically merged into `main`. --- ## 4️⃣ Merge Conflicts (The Final Boss) Conflict is inevitable. A **Merge Conflict** occurs when two engineers edit the *exact same line of code in the exact same file*, and Git cannot mathematically determine whose changes should win. ```javascript // Engineer A's commit: function calculateTax(subtotal) { <<<<<<< HEAD return subtotal * 0.08; // Your local changes ======= return subtotal * 0.12; // GitHub's incoming changes >>>>>>> origin/feature } ``` Git halts the merge and injects these ugly `<<<<<<<` conflict markers directly into your file. Your project is literally broken until you resolve it. **How to Fix It:** 1. Open the conflicted file in VS Code. 2. Look for the conflict markers. VS Code provides helpful buttons: "Accept Current Change", "Accept Incoming Change", or "Accept Both". 3. Manually edit the file until it is correct. Remove the `<<<` and `>>>` symbols. 4. Tell Git you have resolved it by staging the file: `git add file.js`. 5. Finish the merge: `git commit -m "resolve merge conflict"`. --- ## 5️⃣ Advanced Professional Techniques ### `git stash` (The Temporary Pocket) Imagine you are halfway through a complex feature, your code is completely broken, and your boss says: "I need you to fix a typo on the login page right now." You cannot commit broken code, and you cannot checkout a new branch with uncommitted changes. `git stash` takes all uncommitted changes, rips them out of your Working Directory, and tucks them in a temporary hidden pocket. Your project is instantly reverted to a clean state. You fix the typo, commit it, and then run `git stash pop` to dump your messy, half-finished work right back into your lap exactly as you left it. ### `git rebase` vs `git merge` When catching your branch up with `main`: - **`git merge main`**: Takes all new commits from `main`, brings them to your branch, and creates a messy "Merge Commit" tying the histories together like a knot. - **`git rebase main`**: Rewrites history. It unplugs your feature branch, moves the entire base of it to the very tip of `main`, and replays your commits one by one. The resulting history is a perfectly straight, beautiful line with no merge knots. --- ## 💡 Summary Checklist for Teams - [ ] I always run `git pull` before starting work to avoid conflicts later. - [ ] I never commit directly to `main`. I always use an isolated `feature/` branch. - [ ] My commits are granular and have descriptive messages. - [ ] I open a Pull Request for team review before merging to production. - [ ] I know how to manually resolve conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`) when Git gets confused.

questions.question_text: Which command creates a new branch and switches to it?
questions.explanation: Both 'git checkout -b' (classic) and 'git switch -c' (modern) are used specifically for this purpose.

questions.question_text: What command is used to download an existing repository from GitHub to your local machine?
questions.explanation: git clone creates a full copy of the remote repository and its history.

options: A URL pointing to a version of your project on a server (like GitHub)
A branch that has been deleted
questions.explanation: Remotes allow you to sync your local work with a shared repository.

questions.question_text: Which command sends your local commits to GitHub?
questions.explanation: Pushing 'updates' the remote branch with your local changes.

questions.question_text: Which command fetches changes from GitHub AND merges them into your local branch?
questions.explanation: git pull is essentially 'git fetch' followed by 'git merge'.

options: A proposal to merge changes from one branch into another in a collaborative setting
A way to delete a branch
questions.explanation: PRs are the heart of collaboration on platforms like GitHub.

questions.question_text: How do you create a new branch named 'feature-login'?
questions.explanation: 'git branch' creates it. 'git checkout -b' creates it AND switches to it.

options: Git deletes both versions of the file
Git cannot automatically decide which changes to keep and needs a human to fix it
questions.explanation: Conflicts happen when two people change the same line of the same file.

questions.question_text: Which command switches you from the 'main' branch to a 'dev' branch?
questions.explanation: Checkout (or 'git switch') updates your files to match the target branch.

options: Creating a personal copy of someone else's repo on your GitHub account
Running two versions of Git at once
questions.explanation: Forking lets you experiment on a project without affecting the original.

questions.question_text: In a 'Rebase', what happens to your commits?
options: They are moved to the end of the target branch's history
They are sent to the trash
questions.explanation: Rebase creates a cleaner, linear history by moving your base commit.

questions.question_text: What is the standard name of the main remote on most repositories?
questions.explanation: Origin is the default alias for the URL you cloned from.

questions.question_text: How do you delete a local branch named 'old-feature'?
questions.explanation: The '-d' flag safely deletes a branch that has been merged.

questions.question_text: Which GitHub feature allows you to automate tasks like testing or deployment?
questions.explanation: Actions allow you to run 'workflows' on your code automatically.

options: Temporarily hiding uncommitted changes so you can switch branches
questions.explanation: Stashing is like a 'pause button' for your current work.

questions.question_text: Which command is used to see which remotes are connected to your local repo?
questions.explanation: The '-v' (verbose) flag shows the fetch and push URLs.
